segments.s335-g81: که is the relative particle 'who/that' introducing a clause; دانست is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'knew'. جز ... introduces an exception: 'except the king ...'. The epithets پیروز 'victorious' and راد 'noble/generous' follow شاه and together form a noun phrase 'the victorious and noble king' that is the exception to 'who knew'.
segments.s335-g82: که here introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. لهراسب is the proper name (Lohrasp) and دارد is a present-tense possession verb 'has' (classical narrative may use present to state a fact). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and شاهان نژاد is a genitival phrase 'lineage of kings' (شاهان plural + نژاد 'lineage'). Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: verb (دارد) before the governing prepositional phrase.
segments.s335-g83: چو is a conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. سوگند خوردم is the idiom 'to take an oath' in which خوردم is a past-tense first-person verb 'I took/uttered' (classical usage—خوردن here forms the idiom with سوگند). به خاک سیاه is the prepositional phrase 'upon the black earth' (به + noun + adjective), placed after the verb as its complement.
segments.s335-g84: This clause states the result: لب 'lip' is fronted and آلوده شد is the compound predicate 'became stained/tainted' (شد = 'became'). مشمر آن names the person affected ('that Mashmar' — مشمر is a proper name here, with the demonstrative آن), and از گناه ('from/by sin') gives the cause. The order is somewhat compressed/poetic: the possessed notion 'Mashmar's lip' is expressed by juxtaposing لب with the name phrase.
segments.s335-g25: به ایرانیان گفت کز بخت اوی is built as 'he said to the Iranians that...'. به + [people] marks the indirect object ('to the Iranians'); گفت is the past verb 'said' (the subject is omitted and understood from context); کز introduces the content clause 'that'. بخت اوی = 'his fate/fortune' (اوی = his).
segments.s335-g26: بباشید شادان دل از تخت اوی is an imperative/content clause continuing what was said: بباشید is the plural imperative 'be' (addressing the Iranians); شادان دل is a predicate phrase 'joyful of heart' (شادان modifies دل); از تخت اوی literally 'from/of his throne' — here marking the occasion or object of their joy ('at/for his throne').
segments.s335-g27: شگفت اندر او مانده ایرانیان places the predicate ('a wonder remained') before the subject for emphasis. شگفت 'a marvel/wonder' + اندر او 'about/at him' + مانده (past participle) 'remained' — together: 'a wonder remained about him' — followed by the subject ایرانیان ('the Iranians'), yielding 'the Iranians remained in wonder at him.'
segments.s335-g28: برآشفته هر یک چو شیر ژیان: برآشفته is a participial/adjectival form 'stirred up / agitated'; هر یک = 'each one'; چو = comparative 'as/like'; شیر ژیان is a simile 'like a (living/vigorous) lion.' The phrase describes each Iranian being roused, with the simile following the subject.
segments.s335-g29: همی هر کسی در شگفتی بماند: همی + present-subjunctive/long form (بماند) gives a continuous or emphatic sense ('remained/stood'); هر کسی = 'every person / everyone'; در شگفتی = 'in astonishment'. The clause says that everyone continued in wonder.
segments.s335-g30: که لهراسب را شاه بایست خواند is a subordinate clause introduced by که 'that'. لهراسب را شاه خواند is the Persian pattern 'to call X Y' = 'to call Lohrasp king', with را marking the direct object; بایست (archaic/formal) means 'it ought/is fitting' (modal), so together 'that Lohrasp ought to be called king.' خواند here means 'to call/summon' in the sense 'to name/call someone something.'
segments.s335-g31: از آن انجمن زال بر پای خاست is literal and prosaic: از آن انجمن 'from that assembly', زال (name) is the subject, and بر پای خاست literally 'rose onto his feet' = 'stood up / rose to his feet.' خاست is the past tense 'arose'.
segments.s335-g32: بگفت آنچه بودش به دل رای راست: بگفت is the past 'he said'. آنچه 'that which/what' is the object; بودش = بود + -ش enclitic = 'was to him' (i.e. 'was in his possession/mind'), so آنچه بودش به دل = 'what was in his heart'. رای راست 'right counsel' (رای = counsel/plan, راست = true/right) qualifies what he spoke. Word order places the object before the verb, a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s335-g57: که functions as a relative pronoun 'that/who' introducing the clause. یزدان is the subject 'God'. کسی را uses کسی ('someone') with the object/topic marker را making 'someone' definite/targeted. کند is the verb 'makes/causes' (present/factual), and نیک‌بخت is an adjective 'fortunate' describing the result of that action.
segments.s335-g58: سزاوار is an adjective 'deserving/fit' that takes a genitive/nominal complement; شاهی here means 'kingship' (the thing he is deserving of). و links the second complement; زیبای is an adjective 'beautiful', and تخت is 'throne' (the object described by زیبای). Together they state what the person is worthy of.
segments.s335-g59: جهان‌آفرین 'the world‑creator/Creator' is the subject. بر روانم is a prepositional phrase 'upon my soul' (literally 'on my soul') functioning as the thing before which the Creator is witness. گواست is an archaic/poetic copular-verb meaning 'is witness' (here 'testifies' or 'is a witness').
segments.s335-g60: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. گشت is the past/resultive of گشتن 'became/turned out' meaning 'proved/turned out to be'. این سخنها is the subject 'these words', and به + لهراسب marks the person concerned ('of/for Lohrasp'); راست is a predicative adjective 'true'. The clause says these words turned out to be true of Lohrasp.
segments.s335-g61: که introduces the clause 'that ...'. دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/possesses'. همی is a frequentative/adverb 'always/continually' placed before the list of qualities. شرم, دین, و خرد are nouns enumerated as the qualities he possesses: 'modesty/shame, religion/piety, and wisdom'.
segments.s335-g62: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. کردار نیکی is a noun phrase 'good deed(s)/good conduct' (کردار qualified by نیکی). همی again marks habitual action 'continually/always'. برخورد here means 'encounters/receives' (he meets/receives from those good deeds), so the phrase says he continually receives (the results of) good deeds.
segments.s335-g63: نبیره means 'offspring/descendant' and functions as the predicate noun. جهاندار is an epithet 'sovereign' modifying the following name; هوشنگ is the proper name Hushang. هست is the copula 'is', completing the identification 'is the descendant of the sovereign Hushang.'
segments.s335-g64: A simple coordinated adjectival phrase describing him: خردمند 'wise' (adjective); و is the conjunction 'and'; بینادل is an uncommon or uncertain word here (its precise sense is obscure in the manuscript tradition); و again 'and'; پاک‌دست is a compound adjectival epithet 'clean‑handed' meaning honest/upright. The sequence lists three qualities.
segments.s335-g17: سپردم is a first-person past verb ('I entrusted/handed over'). The recipient is marked by the preposition phrase به تو ('to you') placed immediately after the verb; the things given (شاهی و تاج و گنج) follow as direct objects coordinated by و.
segments.s335-g18: از آن پس که ... is a temporal / causal clause meaning 'since/after that (because)'. دیدم is past 'I saw'; بسی here functions as an intensifier 'much' (poetic usage), and درد و رنج is a coordinated noun phrase 'pain and suffering/affliction'.
segments.s335-g19: مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn (away)'. زبان is the object 'tongue/speech'; ز این سپس means 'from this hereafter/thereafter'. جز introduces an exclusion 'except', and بداد here is poetic/fused (see note) meaning 'toward justice' rather than literal 'oppression' in normal vocabulary.
segments.s335-g20: که introduces the reason/purpose clause 'for/so that'. از داد باشی is a causative/instrumental phrase 'by/from justice you be' where باشی is a present-subjunctive/optative form 'may you be' (used here like a jussive), followed by coordinated predicates پیروز و شاد ('victorious and happy').
segments.s335-g21: مکن is negative imperative 'do not'. دیو را uses the object marker را to mark 'the demon' as the direct object. آشنا با روان literally 'acquaint/familiar with the soul' — word order places the adjective/verb-like آشنا and the prepositional phrase با روان after the object.
segments.s335-g22: چو here functions conditionally 'if/when'; خواهی is second-person present 'you want/wish'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that your fortune remain young'. بماند is present-subjunctive/optative 'may remain/last', and جوان qualifies بختت ('your fortune') as 'young/vigorous'.
segments.s335-g23: This is a pair of imperatives: خردمند باش 'be wise' and بی‌آزار باش 'be harmless/inoffensive', linked by و. باش is the imperative 'be', repeated for emphasis after the second predicate.
segments.s335-g24: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. روان را has the object-marking را marking the thing to be protected. نگهدار باش is a periphrastic imperative 'keep / be a keeper' (i.e., 'guard, preserve'); here نگهدار functions imperatively with باش, instructing continuous guarding of the soul.
segments.s335-g65: This clause places the object phrase before the verb in classical Persian order. 'پی جادوان' functions as the thing affected (literally 'the trace/hold of the magicians' or 'the magicians' foothold'), 'بگسلاند' is a past transitive verb (ب‑ + گسلاند) meaning 'he broke/loosened/uprooted', and 'ز خاک' is a prepositional phrase 'from the earth/soil' marking the source from which something was torn away.
segments.s335-g66: 'پدید آورد' is a compound verbal idea: 'پدید' (forth/appear) with the past verb 'آورد' ('brought') = 'brought forth / caused to appear'. 'راه' is the direct object 'way/path', modified by the genitive 'یزدان' ('of God') and the adjective 'پاک' after the noun (postpositive) meaning 'pure'.
segments.s335-g67: The clause has the subject 'زمانه' ('the age/era') followed by the predicate 'جوان گردد' ('becomes young') where 'گردد' is the present/jussive form of 'to become'. The phrase 'از پند اوی' ('from/because of his advice') is a causal adjunct: 'از' = 'because of' and 'اوی' is the archaic possessive 'his'.
segments.s335-g68: Here 'بدین هم' functions as an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this respect / thereby also' (classic 'بـ + دین' or 'به دین' usage). 'بود' is the past copula 'was', followed by 'پاک فرزند اوی' where 'پاک' postpositively qualifies 'فرزند اوی' ('his son') — 'his son was pure/clean in this regard.'
segments.s335-g69: 'به شاهی' indicates the office or status ('to/for kingship'), 'بر او' is the prepositional phrase 'upon him', and 'آفرین گسترید' is a past-tense plural action 'they spread/acclaimed (praise)': 'آفرین' here is a noun 'acclamation/praise' and 'گسترید' is the verb 'they spread/extended' (i.e., they bestowed praise or acclaim).
segments.s335-g70: The clause is an admonition: the archaic preposition 'وز' here functions like 'از' = 'from/of' (introducing 'this advice'); 'این پند و اندرز من' = 'this advice and counsel of mine' (note 'من' is possessive 'my' here), and 'مگذرید' is an imperative/negative sense 'do not let pass / do not disregard'.
segments.s335-g71: 'هر آن کس' = 'every/whichever person', 'کز' is the relative 'who/that', and 'اندرز من درگذشت' means 'passed over / ignored my counsel'. 'درگذشت' here is used in the sense 'passed by / disregarded' (classical verbal usage), not in the sense 'died'.
segments.s335-g72: The clause states a wish/curse: 'همه رنج او' ('all his suffering') is the subject, 'پیش من' ('before me' / 'in my presence') is a locative phrase, and 'باد گشت' combines the optative particle 'باد' ('may') with 'گشت' ('become' / 'come to be') to yield 'may ... come to pass'. In other words, 'may all his suffering come before me.'
segments.s335-g33: چنین گفت is a common poetic sequence meaning 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so' before the verb). The vocative کای + title شهریار بلند follows the verb: کای is the old vocative 'O', شهریار is 'king', and بلند is an attributive adjective 'lofty' placed after the noun for emphasis (poetic word order).
segments.s335-g34: سزد‌گر کنی is an older/poetic conditional-turn: سزدگر (literally 'fitting' or 'if it is fitting') + کنی (2nd person present/subjunctive of کردن 'you do/make') = 'if you deem it fitting / if you do (it) fittingly'. خاک را uses the object marker را to mark 'the earth/dust' as definite, and ارجمند is a predicative adjective 'esteemed/valued' describing what to make the object. The whole clause is an exhortation/conditional request addressed to the king.
segments.s335-g35: سر بخت is a compound (سر + بخت) meaning 'the head/top of fortune' = 'the person's fortune/fate'. آن کس 'that person' follows it (poetic ordering). پر از + noun is the construction 'full of ...' (پر = 'full'), and باد at the end is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical/poetic wish).
segments.s335-g36: روان ورا is an inversion: روان 'soul' with the enclitic/particle ورا meaning 'to him' (his soul). خاک تریاک is a genitive-like apposition 'dust of opium' (تریاک = 'opium' used concretely or figuratively), and again باد is the optative 'may it be.' The whole clause is a parallel wish to the first.
segments.s335-g37: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause 'that / namely'. لهراسب را uses را as the definite/object marker. خواند is past tense 'called/named' (here 'proclaimed/called [him] king'), and به داد 'by/for justice' gives the reason/cause (به + abstract noun داد = 'because of justice').
segments.s335-g38: ز بیداد 'from (or concerning) injustice' (ز is the poetic variant of از). هرگز نگیریم یاد is a negative present/subjunctive pattern meaning literally 'we never take away the remembrance' — idiomatically 'we shall never forget.' نگیریم is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/negative form of گرفتن 'take', and یاد = 'memory/remembrance'.
segments.s335-g39: به ایران چو آمد = 'when he came to Iran' (چو = archaic/poetic 'when'). The following به نزد + name means 'to the presence of' or 'before' (نزد here = 'in the presence of / before'). Word order is typical epic style: temporal clause first, then destination/presence.
segments.s335-g40: فرومایه‌ای is an indefinite noun/adjective 'a low-born/base fellow'. دیدمش is the verb دیدم 'I saw' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' attached (so 'I saw him'). با یک اسب is a simple prepositional phrase 'with one horse' describing the man; با = 'with', یک = 'one'.
segments.s335-g41: به جنگ الانان فرستادیش is built around the past-tense verb فرستاد (sent) with the enclitic pronoun -یش (= him). به + جنگ means 'to/into war'; الانان is the object/target ('the Alans'). Word order is prepositional phrase (to war) + target people + verb+enclitic, so literally 'to the war of the Alans he-sent-him' = 'he sent him to fight the Alans.'
segments.s335-g42: سپاه و درفش و کمر دادیش lists three direct objects joined by و (and) followed by the past verb داد with the same enclitic -یش (gave him). دادیش = داد + -یش = 'gave him'. The nouns are given in a series (army, banner/standard, girdle/belt) — typical Persian coordination before the verb.
segments.s335-g43: ز چندین بزرگان خسرو نژاد uses ز (poetic from/of) + quantified noun چندین بزرگان ('several great ones'). خسرو نژاد is a postpositive modifier meaning 'of/related to Khosrow' or 'kingly/royal-born' (lineage). The whole phrase means 'from/several great men of royal lineage.'
segments.s335-g44: نیامد کسی بر دل شاه یاد is negative past نیامد ('did not come'). کسی here functions as 'anyone/no one' (context gives 'no one'). بر دل شاه is a locative idiom 'upon the king's heart' meaning 'to the king's mind' and یاد ('memory/remembrance') is the syntactic subject/thing that 'did not come' — literally 'no one's remembrance came upon the king's heart.'
segments.s335-g45: نژادش ندانم ندیدم هنر is compressed poetic order: نژادش ('his lineage', with enclitic -ش) + ندانم ('I do not know') — a present negative of دانستن placed before the next clause. Then ندیدم هنر = 'I have not seen (such) excellence/skill.' Note the shift: ندانم is 'I don't know', ندیدم is past negative 'I have not seen'; هنر is the object of ندیدم.
segments.s335-g46: از این‌گونه نشنیده‌ام تاجور: از این‌گونه ('of/from this kind, such') introduces the scope; نشنیده‌ام is perfect/past-negative 'I have not heard of' (shunیدن with first-person singular suffix), and تاجور ('merchant/trader') is the noun qualified by از این‌گونه. The construction means 'I have not heard of a trader of this sort.'
segments.s335-g47: خروشی برآمد ز ایرانیان has the noun خروشی ('a cry/clamor') as subject before the past verb برآمد ('arose/issued forth'), followed by the source phrase ز ایرانیان ('from the Iranians'). This is straightforward subject–verb–source ordering typical in epic diction.
segments.s335-g48: کز این پس نبندیم شاها میان begins with the conjunction کز ('that'), then این پس ('from this time/after this'). نبندیم is first-person plural negative (subjunctive/imperative flavor) of بستن meaning 'we will not close/bind'; شاها is a vocative 'O king'; میان ('between/midst') completes the phrase. The line reports the content of the clamor: 'that from now on, O king, we will not close (it) between (us).'
segments.s335-g73: چنین هم = 'thus/so, too' (adverbial frame). ز یزدان بود ناسپاس is a nominal predicate: ناسپاس 'ungrateful' is predicated by بود (past of بودن) and governed by the preposition ز + یزدان, an older/poetic use meaning roughly 'toward/with respect to God'. Word order (adverbial – prepositional phrase – copula – predicate) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s335-g74: به دلش اندر آید = 'comes into his heart': به ... اندر (into/within) + دلش (heart + 3sg poss. clitic ش) + آید (present/habitual 'comes' used narratively). ز هر سو هراس is a prepositional phrase 'fear from every side' (ز = 'from/of', هر سو = 'every side/direction') modifying the verb.
segments.s335-g75: چو بشنید = temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'); the subject Zāl follows the verb (typical classical word order). این سخنهای پاک = 'these pure words' (این + plural noun سخنهای + adjective پاک; adjective follows the noun).
segments.s335-g76: بیازید انگشت و بر زد به خاک consists of two coordinated actions: بیازید انگشت 'he opened/spread his finger(s)' (بیازید is a classical past-form meaning 'opened/gaped') and و بر زد به خاک 'and struck (it) upon the earth' (زد = past 'struck', بر ... به = onto/to the ground). The object of زد is understood (his finger or hand).
segments.s335-g77: بیالود لب را به خاک سیاه — here بیالود functions as a transitive past verb 'he smeared/soiled' (classical usage) with لب را as the definite object (را marks the object). به خاک سیاه 'with black earth' indicates the material/instrument; خاک سیاه is noun + adjective (adjective after noun).
segments.s335-g78: به آواز لهراسب را خواند شاه = 'in a voice he called Lohrāsb, the king.' به آواز is an adverbial ('with a voice' / 'aloud'), لهراسب را is the definite object (را), خواند (here = 'called/summoned') is the verb in past tense, and شاه is placed after the verb as a nominative/title — typical classical placement where the subject may follow the verb.
segments.s335-g79: به شاه جهان گفت = 'he said to the king of the world' (به + indirect object, شاه جهان as title). گفت introduces the direct speech that follows; خرم بدی here is an optative/wish: classical short formula meaning 'may you be joyful' where بدی is an archaic/poetic optative form rather than the noun 'evil'.
segments.s335-g80: همیشه ز تو دور دست بدی = 'may evil always be far from you.' همیشه 'always', ز تو 'from you', دور 'far', دست بدی 'the hand/power of evil' (دست used literally as 'hand' or figuratively 'power/agency', and بدی here is the noun 'evil'). The whole clause is a continuation of the wishing formula started in the previous segment.
segments.s335-g1: This is a temporal/causal prepositional phrase: ز (poetic 'from/after') + noun phrase کار بزرگان ('the affairs/deeds of the great') followed by چو + past participle/adj پردخته شد ('were arranged/finished'). چو here functions as 'when'. The whole unit means 'when/after the great ones' affairs had been set in order.'
segments.s335-g2: شهنشاه is the subject 'the emperor/king'; زآن + رنجها is a prepositional phrase 'from/of those hardships'; رخته شد is a light-passive idiom ('to be cast off / to be rid of') with شد as auxiliary. Put together: 'the emperor was (thereby) rid of those sufferings.'
segments.s335-g3: از آن مهتران is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from among those chiefs/elders'; نام لهراسب is the noun phrase 'the name/reputation of Lohrasp' and ماند is the simple past 'remained/was left'. Word order places the genitive phrase before the verbless core 'name ... remained.'
segments.s335-g4: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'), followed by از دفتر شاه ('from the king's register/book') and کس (indefinite; here used negatively) + برنخواند, a past negative of برخواندن. برنخواند here means 'did not summon/call'—so the clause explains why the name remained: 'for no one summoned him from the royal register.'
segments.s335-g5: به بیژن بفرمود is a polite/honorific past 'he commanded/told Bijan'; تا introduces purpose ('so that / in order that'); با کلاه is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with a hat' (likely indicating manner or disguise). The clause means 'he told Bijan so that, with a hat, ...'.
segments.s335-g6: بیاورد is a subjunctive/imperative-purpose form 'bring' (dependent on تا + command); لهراسب را is the definite object (را marks the direct object); نزد شاه indicates destination/presence 'before/in the presence of the king'. Word order places the verb before object for emphasis but the intended meaning is 'to bring Lohrasp before the king.'
segments.s335-g7: چو = 'when'; دیدش is the past tense 'saw him' with the object enclitic -ش; جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'; برپای جست is a compound verbal phrase 'leapt to his feet/sprung up' (برپای = 'on foot/upright', جست = 'leaped'). The clause describes the sovereign's immediate physical reaction on seeing him.
segments.s335-g8: بر او آفرین کرد is literal 'upon him he made Afarin' i.e. 'he praised/acclaimed him' (آفرین as an interjection/noun, کرد = 'did/made'); و بگشاد دست is 'and opened/stretched (his) hand'—بگشاد here is a past form meaning 'opened/extended'. The two actions are coordinated: praise followed by a gesture of giving/benediction.
segments.s335-g9: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'descended/ alighted' (فرود + آمد), with آمد as the past-tense verb; the noun فرود appears before آمد as is common in classical Persian compounds. از نامور تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'from the famed ivory throne' where نامور ('renowned') modifies تخت عاج ('ivory throne').
segments.s335-g10: ز سر برگرفت literally 'from the head/top he removed/took' where ز سر is a locative 'from the top' and برگرفت is past tense 'took off/removed' (here meaning removing the crown from the head). آن دل‌افروز تاج is the object 'that heart‑delighting crown.' Word order shows the prepositional phrase before the verb and the object after it, typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s335-g11: به لهراسب بسپرد is a simple clause 'he entrusted to Lohrasp' with به + proper name marking the recipient and بسپرد (past of سپردن) 'entrusted/handed over' as the verb. و کرد آفرین is a coordinated clause: کرد (did/said) + آفرین (acclamation) = 'and he gave praise / exclaimed
segments.s335-g12: همه پادشاهی ایران‌زمین is a noun phrase 'the whole kingdom of Iran' where همه ('whole/entire') modifies پادشاهی ('kingdom/kingship') and ایران‌زمین ('land of Iran') specifies which kingdom. This phrase functions as the direct object of بسپرد (he entrusted/handed over).
segments.s335-g13: همی کرد is an iterative/imperfective past construction meaning 'kept doing' or 'was wont to do' (همی + past-verb). پدرود آن تخت عاج is the object 'a farewell to that ivory throne' (classical Persian often uses پدرود + noun to mean 'farewell to X' without an overt preposition).
segments.s335-g14: بر او آفرین کرد = 'he praised him' where بر + pronoun + آفرین کرد is an idiomatic way to express 'offered praise to him.' The second half, و بر تخت و تاج, omits the verb and repeats the prepositional structure by ellipsis: '(and) (he praised) the throne and the crown' with بر governing تخت و تاج; thus the verb کرد is understood for the final phrase.
segments.s335-g15: که introduces the following blessing clause. این تاج نو بر تو فرخنده باد is an optative clause: فرخنده is a predicate adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' and باد is the optative particle meaning 'may it be.' Word order places the noun phrase (این تاج نو) first, the dative/prepositional phrase (بر تو) next, then the predicate adjective and the optative verb at the end—typical of Persian blessing formulas.
segments.s335-g16: جهان سر به سر is an idiomatic noun phrase 'the whole world, one and all' (سر به سر meaning 'entirely/one and all'). پیش تو ('before you' / 'in your presence') marks the relation, and بنده باد is again the optative 'may be a servant' (بنده 'servant' + باد 'may it be'). The clause is a parallel wish to the prior blessing.
segments.s335-g49: نجوییم is a first-person plural present form built from جویم with the negative prefix ن-: here it means “we do not seek / we will not seek.” The sequence کس نام is the object phrase “anyone's name/fame” (کس = anyone, نام = reputation), and در کارزار is a locative phrase “in battle.” Word order is verb-initial (verb → object → locative), typical of this elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s335-g50: چو introduces a subordinate (temporal/causal) clause here (“when/for”), followed by لهراسب را (Lohrāsb marked with the object/topic marker را). کی is the interrogative “who,” کند is the verb “does/makes,” and شهریار is the role/title “king.” The clause reads as a rhetorical question: “who will make Lohrāsb king?” Note the archaic/poetic ordering where the question word precedes the verb.
segments.s335-g51: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” بشنید is the past tense 3rd singular “he heard.” خسرو is the subject (Khosrow). ز + دستان سخن is a prepositional phrase “from/of the dāstān(s)/tales/words” (ز = from, دستان here = ‘tales/stories’, سخن = ‘words/speech’). The clause thus: “When Khosrow heard the words from the tales…”.
segments.s335-g52: بدو گفت = “he said to him” (بدو = to him; گفت = said). مشتاب here is an admonitive phrase in the imperative sense “do not haste / do not be hasty” (classical usage can present a short negative imperative form); و = “and”; تندی مکن = “do not be violent/impetuous” with مکن the negative imperative. Together: he told him not to hurry and not to be rash.
segments.s335-g53: که هر کس که is the fixed classical pattern ‘whoever’ (literally 'that every person that...') introducing a subordinate clause. هر کس is the subject 'every person/anyone', and بیداد گوید همی is the predicate: بیداد 'injustice' is the object/idea acted on, گوید is the present habitual verb, and همی is the archaic/habitual particle marking continuous or customary action (so the whole phrase means 'whoever is constantly guilty of/does injustice'). The word order is typical older Persian: subject (هر کس) + relative marker (که) + verb phrase, with همی marking habitual aspect.
segments.s335-g54: به جز introduces an exception ('except' / 'other than'); دود ز آتش is a genitive phrase 'smoke from the fire' (ز = from). نجوید is the present/habitual form of 'seek' and together with همی gives a continuous/habitual sense. The construction with به جز + (noun) + verb conveys 'seeks nothing except ...' (i.e., will find/obtain only ...). Word order places the exception phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s335-g55: که is the subordinating 'that' linking this clause to the previous thought. نپسندد is the present form with negative prefix ن- meaning 'does not approve/like'. از ما 'of/from us' marks the source of the evil (i.e., 'our evil'), بدی is 'evil/wrongdoing', and دادگر is an appositive noun 'the just one / just ruler' which here is the logical subject; poetry allows the verb to precede its subject, so the clause reads 'that the just ruler does not approve of our evil.'
segments.s335-g56: نه at the start negates the whole clause ('not'); هر begins 'every' and کاو here functions as a relative 'who/that' (an older/poetic form equivalent to 'که او'), introducing the relative clause. بدی کرد is a past action 'did evil', and بیند is a present/habitual verb 'sees' (classical Persian often mixes forms to express general results), so the clause means 'not everyone who has done evil will see (a) gem.' گهر is the object 'gem' (metaphorical reward).

sentences.s335-s41: who knew, except the victorious and noble king, that Lohrasp was of the royal line.
sentences.s335-s42: When I swore upon the black earth, Mashmar's lip was stained by that sin.
sentences.s335-s13: He told the Iranians that, by his fortune, be joyful of heart at his throne.
sentences.s335-s14: The Iranians were left amazed at him; each one was stirred up like a living lion.
sentences.s335-s15: Everyone remained in astonishment that Lohrasp ought to be called king.
sentences.s335-s16: From that assembly Zāl rose to his feet and spoke what was in his heart — right counsel.
sentences.s335-s29: That God makes someone fortunate and deserving of kingship and a beautiful throne.
sentences.s335-s30: The Creator is witness upon my soul that these words proved true of Lohrasp.
sentences.s335-s31: That he always possesses modesty, piety, and wisdom; from good deeds he continually receives (their effect).
sentences.s335-s32: He is a descendant of the sovereign Hushang; wise, (betweenadel — a rare/uncertain epithet), and clean‑handed.
sentences.s335-s9: I entrusted to you the kingship, the crown, and the treasure, for I had seen so much pain and suffering.
sentences.s335-s10: Do not let your tongue turn away from this hereafter except toward justice, for by justice you may be victorious and glad.
sentences.s335-s11: Do not make the demon familiar with the soul, if you wish that your fortune remain young.
sentences.s335-s12: Be wise and be harmless; always guard/preserve the soul.
sentences.s335-s33: He uprooted the magicians from the soil and brought forth the pure way of God.
sentences.s335-s34: By his counsel the age became youthful; in this respect his son too was pure.
sentences.s335-s35: They acclaimed him to kingship, and do not pass over this my advice and counsel.
sentences.s335-s36: Whoever neglects my counsel — may all his suffering come before me.
sentences.s335-s17: Thus he said, 'O lofty king, if you deem it fitting, make the dust/earth honored.'
sentences.s335-s18: May that man's fortune be full of dust; may his soul, to him, be the dust of opium.
sentences.s335-s19: that he called Lohrāsb king for justice; we will never remove from memory the injustice.
sentences.s335-s20: When he came to Iran and to the presence of Zarasp, I saw a low-born man with a single horse.
sentences.s335-s21: He sent him to war against the Alans, and gave him an army, a standard, and a girdle.
sentences.s335-s22: Of the several great men of kingly stock, no one came to the king's mind.
sentences.s335-s23: His lineage I do not know; I have not seen such excellence, nor have I heard of such a trader.
sentences.s335-s24: A clamor arose from the Iranians: 'From this time forth, O king, we will not keep (things) closed between [us].'
sentences.s335-s37: Thus too he was ungrateful to God; fear came into his heart from every side.
sentences.s335-s38: When Zāl heard these pure words, he opened his fingers and struck the ground.
sentences.s335-s39: He smeared his lip with black earth; in a voice he called Lohrāsb, the king.
sentences.s335-s40: He said to the king of the world, 'May you be joyful; may evil always be far from you.'
sentences.s335-s1: When the affairs of the great were settled, the emperor was rid of those sufferings.
sentences.s335-s2: From among those chiefs Lohrasp's name remained, for no one summoned him from the king's register.
sentences.s335-s3: He ordered Bijan to bring Lohrasp, with a hat, before the king.
sentences.s335-s4: When the sovereign saw him he sprang to his feet, praised him, and stretched out his hand.
sentences.s335-s5: From the famed ivory throne he descended, and from its top he took that heart‑delighting crown.
sentences.s335-s6: He handed (the realm) to Lohrasp and praised him, handing over the entire kingdom of the land of Iran.
sentences.s335-s7: He continued bidding farewell to that ivory throne; he praised him, and (likewise) praised the throne and the crown.
sentences.s335-s8: May this new crown be auspicious upon you; may the whole world, one and all, be a servant before you.
sentences.s335-s25: We seek no one's renown in battle; who will make Lohrāsb king?
sentences.s335-s26: When Khosrow heard the words from those tales, he said to him: “Do not hurry, and do not be impetuous.”
sentences.s335-s27: For whoever continually practices injustice will seek from the fire nothing but smoke.
sentences.s335-s28: That the just ruler does not approve of our evil; not everyone who has done evil will behold a gem.
